title: Compassion Benchmark Daily Briefing — July 12, 2026
headline: Anthropic holds at 59.1 after a Chinese regulator's backdoor allegation fails both tests for a score change.
summary: Anthropic's published score holds at 59.1 out of 100 after a Chinese government cybersecurity platform alleged that its Claude Code tool contains an undisclosed 'backdoor.' The benchmark checked the claim against its own rule for when such a finding can move a score: it needs either a genuine second hidden-tracking episode or a ruling from a neutral regulator or court. Neither condition was met tonight, so the score stays the same, but the watch on Anthropic is now heightened. Tunisia's proposed score cut, from 34.4 to 23.8 out of 100, was reaffirmed for a third straight day after a Tunis court sentenced 21 opposition figures to 12 to 35 years in prison. The published score stays at 34.4 while the benchmark's editors review the proposal. Venezuela's earthquake death toll climbed to 4,333, but the increase reflects the disaster's scale rather than a new government failure, so its score holds at 18 of 100. Israel and Yemen both remain at the lowest possible score, 0 of 100, as fresh evidence of harm keeps arriving. A long-running question about whether Mali and Burkina Faso are scored consistently for similar conduct is now open about nineteen days. Five countries -- Iran, the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Ethiopia, Israel, and Yemen -- confirmed scores so close to zero that even severe new evidence cannot move them further under the benchmark's rules.

pipeline.tunisiaHandling: Tunisia's proposed score of 23.8 of 100 (down from a published 34.4) remains a proposal only, reaffirmed for a third cycle after a fresh mass-sentencing finding. The published score stays at 34.4 while the benchmark's editors review the finding. No change has been made to the live index.

topSignals.title: Anthropic Holds at 59.1 as a Chinese Regulator's Backdoor Claim Fails the Benchmark's Own Test
topSignals.whyItMatters: A rival government alleged hidden tracking in a US AI product. The benchmark checked the claim against its own rules and found it did not clear the bar for a score change.
topSignals.description: Anthropic holds a published score of 59.1 out of 100, in the Functional band. Between July 8 and 10, a cybersecurity platform run by China's Ministry of Industry and Information Technology posted an alert claiming that Anthropic's Claude Code tool contains a 'backdoor' that sends a user's location and identity to a remote server without consent. Anthropic said the mechanism was an anti-piracy and anti-copying check, deployed earlier in 2026, and that its removal was already scheduled for a July 2 software update -- before the Chinese alert appeared. The benchmark has a standing rule for this kind of claim: a score can only move if there is either a genuine second hidden-tracking episode, separate from one already priced into the score, or a finding from a neutral regulator or a court. Neither condition was met. This is best read as a rival government's relabeling of a mechanism Anthropic had already disclosed and scheduled for removal, not a new hidden episode, and an allegation by a foreign government agency is not the same as a court or regulator ruling. The underlying conduct -- sending location and identity data without clear consent -- is real, but it is largely the same conduct already counted against Anthropic's score months ago. The published score holds at 59.1, and the watch on Anthropic is now heightened. Confidence: high.

evidence.claim: China's Ministry of Industry and Information Technology issued a security alert naming Claude Code versions 2.1.91 through 2.1.196 and urging users to uninstall or upgrade.
Anthropic said the mechanism was an anti-distillation and anti-abuse check, not a surveillance backdoor, and that removal was already scheduled for the July 2 release.

topSignals.title: Tunisia's Pending Score Cut Is Reaffirmed for a Third Day as 21 Opposition Figures Are Sentenced
topSignals.whyItMatters: New evidence supports a score cut proposed two days earlier, but the published score does not move until the benchmark's editors act on it.
topSignals.description: Tunisia holds a published score of 34.4 out of 100, in the Developing band. A review proposed on July 10 suggested a lower score of 23.8 out of 100, based on the 25-year prison sentence given to Sihem Bensedrine, the former head of Tunisia's own Truth and Dignity Commission, and the mass expulsion of more than 12,000 migrants. Tonight, a Tunis court sentenced 21 more people -- including leaders of the Ennahda party, former government officials, and lawyers -- to prison terms of 12 to 35 years on vague security charges. Rached Ghannouchi, Ennahda's former president, received a 14-year sentence in absentia, the latest in a string of convictions against him. This new sentencing matches the evidence already behind the 23.8 score, so the proposed figure is unchanged. No second, duplicate proposal was opened. The published score of 34.4 stays in place while the benchmark's editors review the finding for a third consecutive night. Confidence: medium.

evidence.quote: Former truth commission president Sihem Bensedrine sentenced to 25 years in prison
evidence.claim: Tunisia is imprisoning the leader of its own past accountability body, reversing transitional justice.

evidence.quote: A Tunis court sentenced 21 people including Ennahda party leaders, former government officials and lawyers to prison terms of 12 to 35 years
evidence.claim: A fresh mass sentencing of opposition figures matches the evidence already behind the proposed 23.8 score.

topSignals.title: Venezuela's Earthquake Death Toll Passes 4,300 Without Crossing the Score-Change Line
topSignals.whyItMatters: A rising death toll from a natural disaster is not, by itself, evidence of government misconduct, so Venezuela's score stays the same even as the toll grows.
topSignals.description: Venezuela holds a published score of 18 out of 100, in the Critical band. The death toll from the June 24 twin earthquakes reached 4,333 as of July 11, up from about 3,889 the day before, with nearly 17,000 injured. The US Geological Survey has said the final toll will most likely land somewhere between 10,000 and 100,000, given the scale of destruction. Most of that increase reflects continued recovery of bodies from collapsed buildings, which the benchmark treats as a consequence of the disaster itself, not as new evidence of government failure. Genuine government-conduct concerns -- inadequate shelters, a very high public disapproval rating, and street protests demanding new elections -- continue to build, but they are a deepening of problems already counted, not a new discrete failure. Recalculating Venezuela's disaster-response score for tonight's evidence would land somewhere in the low negative 3-point range, short of the 5-point shift the benchmark requires before changing a published score. Confidence: high.

evidence.claim: Venezuela's earthquake death toll climbed to 4,333 as of July 11, up from roughly 3,889 the day before, with 16,740 people injured.

topSignals.title: Israel and Yemen Both Hold at Zero as New Evidence of Harm Continues to Build
topSignals.whyItMatters: Both countries already sit at the lowest possible score. Tonight's evidence cannot lower either score further, but each event reinforces why the score sits at the floor.
topSignals.description: Israel holds at 0 of 100, the lowest possible score. Israeli forces killed five more Palestinians, including a child, on July 12, despite a ceasefire that has been in effect since October 2025. The cumulative toll since the ceasefire began has reached at least 1,098 killed and 3,535 wounded, and Israeli forces still control roughly 70 percent of the Gaza Strip. Yemen also holds at 0 of 100. The largest prisoner exchange of the war, involving roughly 1,750 detainees, remains collapsed after its planned July 10 start date was postponed. Yemen's UN special envoy said on July 11 that he had received renewed commitments from both sides, but the exchange has not resumed, and opposition from a dissolved southern political council continues to complicate it. Neither score can move lower, since both are already at the floor. Confidence: high for both.

evidence.claim: Israeli army fire killed five Palestinians, including a child, on July 12, despite the ceasefire in effect since October 2025; the cumulative toll since the ceasefire began has reached at least 1,098 killed and 3,535 wounded.
evidence.source: Middle East Monitor, via Gaza Ministry of Health

evidence.claim: The planned exchange of roughly 1,750 detainees, the largest of the conflict, remained postponed after Yemen's UN special envoy reported renewed but unfulfilled commitments from both sides on July 11.

topSignals.title: The Mali-Burkina Faso Scoring Question Reaches Roughly Nineteen Days Open
topSignals.whyItMatters: A long-running question about whether two neighboring countries are scored consistently for similar conduct still has no answer, now the benchmark's longest-open question.
topSignals.description: Mali holds at 12.5 out of 100 and Burkina Faso holds at 6.3 out of 100, both in the Critical band. Tonight, Mali confirmed after a coordinated attack by two armed groups continued for six days at one military base near Anefis; Burkina Faso confirmed after at least 22 soldiers and civilian militia were killed in a weekend attack near Dedougou. In both cases, the dominant source of harm is armed non-state groups, not the governments' own conduct, so neither score moves. The open question is different: whether Mali's score of 12.5 is fairly calibrated against Burkina Faso's 6.3, given that both governments face comparable armed-group violence and comparable documented abuses by their own forces. That question has now gone without a decision for roughly nineteen consecutive days, the longest-running open item the benchmark is tracking, and it remains a matter for the benchmark's coordinating team rather than a single night's assessment. Confidence: high.

evidence.claim: JNIM and an allied Tuareg armed group renewed a coordinated offensive from July 4, with fighting at Anefis continuing for six consecutive days.
At least 22 soldiers and civilian militia were killed in a suspected JNIM attack on a military base near Dedougou over the weekend of July 4-5.

topSignals.title: Five Countries Confirm Near Zero as Tonight's Evidence Cannot Push Any of Them Lower
topSignals.whyItMatters: Iran, the Democratic Republic of the Congo, and Ethiopia all sit so close to the lowest possible score that even severe new evidence has almost nowhere left to move them.
topSignals.description: Iran holds at 2.5 out of 100. Its own conduct -- political executions at a multi-decade high, alongside a collapsing ceasefire in which the United States struck Iran again and Iran's retaliation reportedly hit sites in four Gulf states -- intensifies an already-documented pattern, but a change of five points or more is arithmetically impossible from a score this close to zero. The strikes on Iran are scored against the United States' own conduct, not against Iran. The Democratic Republic of the Congo holds at 2.3 out of 100. Its Ebola outbreak has reached 625 deaths and 1,792 cases, the deadliest Bundibugyo-strain outbreak on record, but most of that toll reflects the epidemic itself rather than new government failure; the government's earlier redeployment of troops away from the outbreak zone remains priced into the near-floor score. Ethiopia holds at 4.7 out of 100. Tension with Eritrea over Red Sea port access continues to build, with troop movements on both sides, but no single new clash was confirmed within the past two weeks. Confidence: high for all three.

evidence.claim: At least 39 people have been executed on politically motivated charges since February 28, 2026, following unfair trials tainted by torture allegations.
The 2026 Bundibugyo Ebola outbreak reached 625 deaths and 1,792 confirmed cases as of July 9, the deadliest Bundibugyo-strain outbreak on record.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Due-process restoration, civil-society reinstatement, and reversal of the indefinite re-election amendment remain the path back toward 20 of 100. The 2027 election under the amendment is the next scheduled test.
boundaryWatch.note: El Salvador sits at 15.0 of 100 in the Critical band, 5.0 points below the boundary with the Developing band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A confirmed enforcement action under the Ethnic Unity Law's diaspora-liability clause -- a prosecution, forced return, or family detention abroad -- is the next threshold event to watch.
boundaryWatch.note: China sits at 19.5 of 100, half a point inside the Critical band. The Ethnic Unity Law and Hong Kong national-security enforcement remain fully priced; no newly and reliably dated escalation surfaced beyond the already-confirmed record.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A second undisclosed telemetry episode, evidence the removed tracker was broader or retained longer than stated, or a regulator or court finding of deceptive practice would each independently move the score toward a change. The EU AI Act becomes fully applicable August 2.
boundaryWatch.note: Anthropic sits at 59.1 of 100, 0.9 points below the Established band. A Chinese government cybersecurity alert claiming a Claude Code 'backdoor' did not meet either prong of the conversion trigger; the watch is now heightened, meaning a future independently-verified recurrence or a neutral finding would be weighed more readily.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A labor board ruling on the unfair-labor-practice charge tied to Apple's closure of its first unionized store is the next scored event to watch.
boundaryWatch.note: Apple sits at 59.4 of 100, 0.6 points below the Established band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A durable ceasefire combined with the Lebanese Armed Forces taking sovereign control of southern Lebanon and delivering reconstruction would together move Lebanon toward 20 of 100.
boundaryWatch.note: Lebanon sits at 17.2 of 100, 2.8 points below the boundary with the Developing band. Fresh Israeli strikes on July 10 and July 12 in southern Lebanon remain the dominant source of harm this window, not Lebanon's own conduct.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Independently verified deliberate targeting of civilians in a cross-border strike would move Pakistan's score down; durable relief for detained protesters and disappeared Balochistan residents would move it up.
boundaryWatch.note: Pakistan sits at 17.2 of 100, 2.8 points below the Developing band. A Balochistan security operation reports a cumulative 102 alleged militants killed as of July 12, atop the already-priced enforced-disappearance pattern; the figure is security-source-reported and not independently verified.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Independent confirmation of a discrete own-conduct failure, such as documented aid diversion or denial of responder access, is the next event that would move Venezuela's score down.
boundaryWatch.note: Venezuela sits at 18.0 of 100. The earthquake death toll reached 4,333, but the increase is attributed to disaster scale, not new government conduct; the evidenced decline remains in the low negative 3-point range, short of the 5-point threshold.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A formal famine declaration in Borno state combined with documented aid obstruction is the next scored event to watch.
boundaryWatch.note: Nigeria sits at 18.0 of 100, 2.0 points below the Developing band. The July 2 food-security record (36.2 million people food insecure nationally) remains operative, continuing the already-priced June 19 downgrade.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented government obstruction of World Health Organization response corridors would be the trigger to move toward the absolute floor. The natural seasonal peak for this outbreak runs through July 31.
boundaryWatch.note: DRC sits at 2.3 of 100, 2.3 points above the absolute floor. The Ebola outbreak reached 625 deaths and 1,792 cases as of July 9, the deadliest Bundibugyo-strain outbreak on record, but the toll is largely externally inflicted.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented expulsions of Bengali Muslim and Rohingya populations, and continued digital surveillance targeting minority groups, remain under watch. Not assessed tonight.
boundaryWatch.note: India sits at 15.6 of 100, 0.6 points above the absolute floor. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A formal famine declaration covering Buur Hakaba or Baidoa, combined with documented government aid obstruction, is the next scored trigger. The famine-risk window runs through September 2026.
boundaryWatch.note: Somalia sits at 4.7 of 100. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented discriminatory denial of emergency protection to stateless bidoon or migrant workers during wartime would move Kuwait's score down; durable expansion of bidoon rights would move it up.
boundaryWatch.note: Kuwait sits at 25.0 of 100, 5.0 points above the Critical band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A coordinator-level review comparing Mali's conduct against Burkina Faso's (6.3 of 100) is the next scored event needed to resolve this gap. No review date has been set.
boundaryWatch.note: Mali sits at 12.5 of 100. A coordinated armed-group offensive continued for six days at one base near Anefis. The calibration question against Burkina Faso has now been open roughly nineteen consecutive days, with no resolution timeline set.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A coordinator-level review comparing Burkina Faso's conduct against Mali's (12.5 of 100) is the next scored event needed. No review date has been set.
boundaryWatch.note: Burkina Faso sits at 6.3 of 100. At least 22 soldiers and civilian militia were killed in a weekend attack near Dedougou. The calibration question against Mali remains open roughly nineteen consecutive days.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A coordinator-level review comparing Bolivia's conduct profile, as an elected government under economic and civil-unrest strain, against Critical-band peers facing state collapse or mass atrocity is the next event needed. No review date has been set.
boundaryWatch.note: Bolivia sits at 6.3 of 100. Not assessed tonight; the calibration question against Burkina Faso and Somalia remains open.

boundaryWatch.trigger: The outcome of the Justice Department's False Claims Act complaint or the Barrows v. Humana class action over AI-driven claim denials would confirm the current Developing-band placement either way.
boundaryWatch.note: Humana sits at 35.2 of 100, 4.8 points below the Functional band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Forced cuts that measurably reduce student or staff support would count against the score; abandoning its academic-freedom stance would as well.
boundaryWatch.note: Princeton sits at 57.8 of 100, 2.2 points below the Established band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Independently documented deliberate targeting of Palestinian civilians by Palestinian authorities themselves, or systematic diversion of aid, would move the score down.
boundaryWatch.note: Palestine sits at 25.0 of 100, 5.0 points above the Critical band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented atrocities by Haitian state security forces, or deliberate relief obstruction, would move the score down. Verified state-led civilian protection would move it up.
boundaryWatch.note: Haiti sits at 4.7 of 100. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A fourth nationwide blackout, or a documented healthcare-access failure beyond tens of thousands of canceled surgeries, is the next event that would move Cuba's score toward a formal change.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A discrete new harm category beyond documented opposition and press suppression is the next event that would move Turkey's score further down.
boundaryWatch.note: Turkey sits at 10.3 of 100. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A return to lethal crackdown tactics, such as the 41 protester deaths recorded during the 2025 Saba Saba march, would be the next event that moves Kenya's score down toward the Critical band.
boundaryWatch.note: Kenya sits at 35.9 of 100. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A documented own-conduct deterioration in epidemic response, such as government obstruction of WHO response corridors, would be the next event that could push Uganda back into the Critical band.
boundaryWatch.note: Uganda sits at 20.3 of 100, just 0.3 points above the Critical band. Surveillance-driven containment of a Marburg case and 20 Ebola spillover cases is a genuine but non-structural operational positive; own-conduct governance concerns are unchanged.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A merits ruling or verdict for the states, a material settlement, or new dated evidence of youth-safety harm or internal-findings suppression is the next event that would move Meta's score.
boundaryWatch.note: Meta Platforms sits at 7.8 of 100, near the bottom of the Fortune 500 list. Not assessed tonight.

emergingRisks.risk: A future independently-verified recurrence of Anthropic's telemetry mechanism, or a neutral regulator or court finding, would convert tonight's heightened watch into a scorable event.
emergingRisks.detail: Tonight's Chinese-government allegation did not meet either prong of the conversion trigger, but the watch on Anthropic is now heightened rather than routine, meaning future evidence will be weighed more readily.

emergingRisks.risk: Tunisia's pending score-review proposal has now gone three full cycles without a decision from the benchmark's editors, even as corroborating evidence continues to accumulate.
emergingRisks.detail: The proposed 23.8 of 100 stays within the Developing band and is not yet applied to the published record. Continued corroboration without action keeps the published score at a value the evidence no longer fully supports.

emergingRisks.risk: Venezuela's earthquake-response score has compounded across several consecutive cycles without crossing the 5-point change threshold.
emergingRisks.detail: The evidenced own-conduct decline has deepened toward the low negative 3-point range. A discrete own-conduct failure, not further toll growth, is the trigger that would move this to a scored change.

emergingRisks.risk: The Mali-Burkina Faso scoring-consistency question is now the longest-open item the benchmark is tracking, at roughly nineteen consecutive days.
emergingRisks.detail: Both countries confirmed again tonight with non-state armed groups as the dominant source of harm, leaving the underlying calibration question about their government-conduct scores unresolved.

emergingRisks.risk: Ethiopia-Eritrea tension continues to build without a single confirmed dated clash, leaving an open question about when sustained pressure becomes a scorable escalation.
emergingRisks.detail: Troop movements and diplomatic tension around Red Sea port access have been documented for multiple cycles, but the 14-day evidence window has not yet captured a single new clash.

emergingRisks.risk: A cluster of near-zero scores (Iran, the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Ethiopia, Israel, and Yemen) means severe new evidence in any of these countries can no longer register as a public score change.
emergingRisks.detail: Five of tonight's fifteen assessed entities are at or within five points of the absolute floor, illustrating how the benchmark's near-floor arithmetic can outpace even severe escalations.

sectorAlerts.sector: AI Labs -- A Rival Government's Allegation Tested Against the Conversion Trigger
sectorAlerts.headline: Anthropic holds at 59.1 of 100 after a Chinese regulator's backdoor claim fails both tests for a score change.
observations: Anthropic (59.1 of 100): a Chinese government cybersecurity alert claimed Claude Code contains an undisclosed backdoor; Anthropic said the mechanism was an anti-distillation check already scheduled for removal on July 2.
Neither prong of the conversion trigger was met: no genuine second hidden-tracking episode, and no ruling from a neutral regulator or court.
The watch on Anthropic is now heightened rather than routine, ahead of the EU AI Act becoming fully applicable on August 2.

sectorAlerts.sector: North Africa -- Tunisia's Pending Review Reaffirmed for a Third Day
sectorAlerts.headline: A fresh mass sentencing of opposition figures matches, without changing, Tunisia's proposed score of 23.8 of 100.
observations: Tunisia (34.4 of 100 published; 23.8 of 100 proposed): a Tunis court sentenced 21 more opposition figures to 12-35 years, including a 14-year sentence in absentia for Rached Ghannouchi.
The published score remains 34.4 of 100 while the benchmark's editors review the proposal for a third cycle.
No duplicate proposal was opened; the new evidence was logged against the existing file.

The evidenced own-conduct decline deepens modestly, toward the low negative 3-point range, but stays short of the 5-point change threshold.
Government-conduct concerns -- inadequate shelters, high public disapproval, and protests -- continue but are a deepening of already-counted problems.

Yemen (0 of 100): the largest prisoner exchange of the war remains collapsed despite reported renewed commitments.

Iran (2.5 of 100): executions reach a multi-decade high, but a five-point score change is arithmetically impossible this close to zero; separately, the US-Iran ceasefire collapse is scored against the United States, not Iran.
Lebanon (17.2 of 100): fresh Israeli strikes on July 10 and July 12 remain the dominant source of harm, not Lebanon's own conduct.

methodologyNotes.name: An Allegation Is Not the Same as a Finding
methodologyNotes.description: When a government agency alleges wrongdoing by a company or another country, the benchmark treats that as an allegation, not as proof, unless a neutral regulator or a court confirms it. Tonight, a Chinese government cybersecurity platform alleged Anthropic's Claude Code contains a hidden backdoor. Because the claim came from a single government agency with its own interests in the dispute, and no neutral body had ruled on it, the benchmark did not treat it as confirmed misconduct. Anthropic's score held at 59.1 of 100, with a heightened watch for any independent confirmation.

methodologyNotes.name: A Pending Proposal Can Be Reaffirmed Without Changing Its Number
methodologyNotes.description: When new evidence supports a score-change proposal that is already awaiting review, the benchmark records the new evidence against the existing proposal rather than opening a second, overlapping one. Tonight, a fresh mass sentencing of 21 opposition figures in Tunisia matched a proposal made two days earlier. The proposed score of 23.8 out of 100 stayed the same; the published score remains 34.4 while the benchmark's editors review the finding.

methodologyNotes.name: A Natural Disaster's Rising Toll Is Not, by Itself, New Misconduct
methodologyNotes.description: When a death toll from an earthquake, flood, or similar disaster keeps climbing, the benchmark separates deaths caused by the disaster's scale from deaths caused by a government's own failure to respond. Venezuela's earthquake toll passed 4,300 tonight, but most of the increase reflects continued recovery of bodies from collapsed buildings, not a new government failure, so the score held.

methodologyNotes.name: A Score Near Zero Has Little Room Left to Fall
methodologyNotes.description: Iran's score of 2.5 out of 100 is so close to the lowest possible mark that even a severe increase in documented executions cannot produce the 5-point change the benchmark requires before adjusting a published score. The same is true for the Democratic Republic of the Congo (2.3) and Ethiopia (4.7). The benchmark records these as near-floor confirmations rather than treating the arithmetic limit as evidence that the underlying conduct does not matter.

forwardTriggers.trigger: The natural seasonal peak for the current Ebola outbreak closes. Documented government obstruction of WHO response corridors is the trigger toward the absolute floor.
The EU AI Act becomes fully applicable. A second undisclosed telemetry episode or a regulator finding of deceptive practice would be the next event that could move the score.
The EU AI Act becomes fully applicable. No public compliance roadmap has been published yet.
The famine-risk window closes. A formal famine declaration combined with documented government aid obstruction would move the score.
The 2027 election under the July 2025 indefinite re-election amendment. Restoring due process and reinstating civil society are the conditions that would move the score back up.

The benchmark's editors act on the proposed 23.8 of 100 downgrade, now reaffirmed for a third cycle. Release of detained rights defenders or reinstatement of the shuttered information-access authority would be conditions for an upgrade instead.
Coordinator-level review of Mali's score against Burkina Faso's for comparable military-government conduct. Roughly nineteen days open with no set review date.
The same coordinator-level review, from Burkina Faso's side of the calibration question.
Coordinator-level review of whether Bolivia's score is calibrated consistently against Critical-band peers facing very different conditions. No review date has been set.
A documented mass-execution event or formal international atrocity finding is the next event that could move Iran's score toward the absolute floor.
Independent verification of any renewed attempt at the collapsed prisoner exchange is the next event that would allow a scored assessment.
First confirmed enforcement action under the Ethnic Unity Law's diaspora-liability clause -- a prosecution, forced return, or family detention abroad.

Independent confirmation of a discrete own-conduct failure, such as documented aid diversion or denial of responder access, is the next event that would move the score toward a formal change from 18.0 of 100.
A confirmed dated clash along the Tigray-Eritrea border is the next event that could move the score toward the floor.

dailyOpeningQuestion.text: Anthropic's heightened downward watch survived a rival government's unadjudicated backdoor allegation tonight; does a future independently-verified recurrence of the same anti-distillation mechanism trigger a score change on its own, or does the conversion trigger require a neutral regulator or court finding specifically?
